prompt: This loop should visit every byte value 0–255 once, then stop. On hardware it never exits. Why?
choices: process() modifies i somewhere by reference
uint8_t wraps to 0 after 255, so i <= 255 is always true
The compiler optimizes the loop away
reveal: A uint8_t holds 0–255. After i = 255, i++ wraps back to 0 — the condition i <= 255 can never be false, so the loop runs forever.
lesson: Make the loop counter wider than the range it counts (here uint16_t), or restructure the exit test. Off-by-one and overflow bugs hide in the variable type, not just the comparison.
caption: Looks fine. Runs forever. Spot why 👇

prompt: An indicator LED is driven straight from a 5 V GPIO pin. It flashes bright, then goes dark for good. What is missing?
snippet:
5V GPIO ───▶|─── GND
            (LED)

   no series resistor in the path
choices: The GPIO cannot source enough current
A flyback diode across the LED
reveal: With no series resistor, nothing limits the current except the LED and source impedance — well past the LED rating. It overheats and fails (often after one bright flash).
lesson: Every LED on a voltage source needs current limiting. Red LED (Vf ≈ 2 V) at 10 mA on 5 V: R = (5 − 2) / 0.01 = 300 Ω → use 330 Ω (E12).
caption: One bright flash, then dead. The fix is one part.

prompt: An I²C sensor works on the bench jig but returns nothing on the new board. The only schematic change: two resistors were removed. Why does the bus fail?
snippet:
3V3 ─────────────── SDA ── MCU
                    SCL ── MCU

  [SENSOR] on SDA/SCL — no resistors to 3V3
choices: I²C lines need pull-up resistors to idle high
The sensor is missing a decoupling capacitor
The MCU pins must be configured push-pull
reveal: I²C is open-drain: devices can only pull the line low, never drive it high. Without pull-ups to Vcc the lines never return high, so there are no valid logic levels and the bus is stuck.
lesson: Open-drain buses (I²C, 1-Wire, SMBus) require pull-ups to set the high state. ~4.7 kΩ to Vcc is the common default; go lower for fast-mode or long/capacitive traces, higher to save power.
caption: Works on the jig, dead on the board. Two missing parts.
